_meta.title: CR new-news — $110M Tried to Pick Your Texas Senator (and lost)
_meta.purpose: FEC-verified dissection of the 2026 TX U.S. Senate super-PAC money, v9 TIGHT cut (3:05). Documentary structure: NEWS -> PUNCHLINE -> EXPLAIN, no mystery-tease. The biggest checks lost (Paxton won ~64% despite $39.3M against him; both winners beat the money that backed them), and the pass-through disguise is the back half. Remotion explainers + 7 reused AI plates (looped) on narrative beats.
_meta.render_doctrine: VO-ANCHORED. Every beat's duration = (next beat's VO start) - (this beat's VO start), summing to 185.28s (= VO length per vo-word-timings.json). News+punchline live in the opening ~30s (Paxton's 64% win lands at 24.70s). The 7 reused AI plates confirmed present in remotion/public/ai-plates/cr-tx-senate/ go on TextSlate narrative beats with backgroundVideo + scrimOpacity 0.55 + backgroundVideoDurationInFrames 301 so they LOOP (no freeze). Remotion CountUp/MoneyFlow/Timeline for info beats; static CountUp number (no roll). ONE caricature per beat, on the beat that names that figure. Newsletter NOT called free; outro campaignreceipts.com.
_meta.overlap_fix: Every CountUp uses a short (<=3 word) label above the big number; the descriptive sentence lives in the caption below. No two text layers share vertical space. One face per beat.

beats.label: Scene 2 — John Nau: the $39.3M bet that lost
Scene 3 — Reid Hoffman: a California check in a Texas race
Scene 5 — What it means + where to read it

clips.covers_script_section: S1 open — most expensive Senate race in the country; $110M outside money into one Texas seat

props.title: The most expensive Senate race
props.body: This was the most expensive Senate race in the country this year. One hundred ten million dollars in outside money poured into one Texas seat.

clips.covers_script_section: S1 'Most of it came from two billionaires.'

props.label: Outside money
props.caption: Most of it came from two billionaires.

clips.covers_script_section: S1 'The biggest checks lost. One super PAC — a group that can spend unlimited money on ads — spent $39.3M to beat a Republican named Ken Paxton.'

props.label: Against Paxton
props.caption: The biggest checks lost. One super PAC — a group that can spend unlimited money on ads — spent this much to beat a Republican named Ken Paxton.

clips.covers_script_section: S1 PUNCHLINE (front-loaded) — 'Paxton won anyway, with about sixty-four percent of the vote.'

props.label: Paxton won
props.caption: Paxton won anyway — with about sixty-four percent of the vote.

clips.covers_script_section: S1 close — 'So the money lost. Let us walk the receipts. They are public, and they show who paid and why.'

props.title: The money lost
props.body: Let us walk the receipts. They are public, and they show who paid and why.

clips.covers_script_section: S2 'Start with the biggest check on the Republican side. His name is John Nau. He is a Houston beer billionaire.'

props.title: John Nau, of Houston
props.body: Start with the biggest check on the Republican side. John Nau is a Houston beer billionaire.

clips.covers_script_section: S2 'FEC filings show he gave $7.9M to a super PAC that backed Senator John Cornyn, the Republican who already held the seat.'

props.label: John Nau gave
props.caption: To a super PAC that backed Senator John Cornyn — the Republican who already held the seat.

clips.covers_script_section: S2 WHY — 'Why would Nau care this much? Nau was Cornyn's finance chairman — the man who raised money for him for years.'

props.title: He was Cornyn’s money man
props.body: Nau was Cornyn’s finance chairman — the man who raised money for him for years.

clips.covers_script_section: S2 'So his PAC spent $39.3M against Cornyn's biggest rival, Ken Paxton.'
S2 PUNCHLINE — 'It did not work. The largest wall of money in the race was built to stop one man, and that man won anyway.'

props.label: It did not work
props.caption: The largest wall of money in the race was built to stop one man — and that man won anyway.

clips.covers_script_section: S3 'Now cross to the Democratic side. One donor is Reid Hoffman. He co-founded LinkedIn, and he is a tech billionaire who lives in California — not Texas.'

props.title: Reid Hoffman, of California
props.body: Now cross to the Democratic side. Reid Hoffman co-founded LinkedIn. He is a tech billionaire who lives in California — not Texas.

clips.covers_script_section: S3 'FEC filings show he gave $1.5M to a super PAC called Lone Star Rising. That group backed James Talarico, and it spent $8.95M against his rival, Jasmine Crockett.'

source.name: Reid Hoffman
source.sublabel: Co-founded LinkedIn · lives in California, not Texas

clips.covers_script_section: S3 WHY — 'Why would a California tech billionaire spend on a Texas seat? As a state lawmaker, Talarico wrote bills to put rules on social media and on fake A-I videos — rules that reach Silicon Valley.'

props.title: Rules that reach Silicon Valley
props.body: As a state lawmaker, Talarico wrote bills to put rules on social media and on fake A-I videos — rules that reach Silicon Valley. A tech billionaire’s interest in that race is no surprise.

clips.covers_script_section: S3 HONEST COMPLICATION — 'But Talarico has never cast a Senate vote, so what Hoffman gets back is still an open question.'

props.title: No one knows yet how he would vote
props.body: Talarico has never cast a single Senate vote. We know Hoffman is paying. What he gets back is still an open question.

clips.covers_script_section: S4 'Some of this money is hidden on purpose.'

props.title: Some of this money is hidden on purpose
props.body: Shell groups stand between the donor and the PAC — so the original name never appears.

clips.covers_script_section: S4 'Five and a half million dollars reached the pro-Cornyn PAC through a shell group called Ohio Works. Three and three-quarter million dollars reached the pro-Talarico PAC through Government That Works.'

props.title: The money came through a middle name

source.name: Donors the law lets stay secret

clips.covers_script_section: S4 PUNCHLINE — 'The original donors paid through middle groups so their names never appear. The law lets them stay secret. Both sides did it. The receipt shows the middle group, never the person who actually wrote the check.'

props.title: The receipt names the messenger, not the sender
props.body: The law lets the original donors stay secret. The receipt shows the middle group — never the person who actually wrote the check.

clips.covers_script_section: S5 'So here is what this race shows. The money was louder than ever, and the group names were friendlier than ever.'

props.title: Louder money, friendlier names
props.body: The money was louder than ever, and the group names were friendlier than ever.

clips.covers_script_section: S5 'But both winners beat the money that backed them. A billionaire can spend until the ads never stop, but he still gets only one vote. This year, the votes beat the checkbook on both sides.'

props.title: One vote each
props.subtitle: TX U.S. Senate 2026 · FEC-verified

rows.name: A billionaire
rows.policy: Can spend until the ads never stop — still gets just one vote.

props.footer: Both winners beat the money that backed them. This year, the votes beat the checkbook on both sides.

clips.covers_script_section: S5 close — 'You can read the full receipt and get the newsletter at campaignreceipts.com.'

props.cta: Read the full receipt and get the newsletter.
